text: The surgeon performs a single-incision laparoscopic proctocolectomy with ilioanal pouch anastomosis and diverting loop ileostomy. After inducing general anesthesia, the patient is positioned in split-leg lithotomy. A single-port device is inserted through an incision on the right side of the umbilicus. Using a 10mm, 30-degree bariatric scope, the abdominal cavity is examined, and the patient is placed in Trendelenburg position with the right side elevated. The cecum is grasped with a Babcock clamp and retracted toward the midline, exposing the vascular plane between the mesenteries of the right colon, ileum, and retroperitoneal structures. The right ureter is identified, and dissection of the appendix, cecum, ascending colon, and ileal mesentery from retroperitoneal structures is performed using endo-scissors connected to electrocautery. Dissection continues up to the third and proximal part of the fourth portion of the duodenum, with care taken to avoid damaging the right external iliac vessels and inferior vena cava.

The surgeon dissects the ascending colon along the white line of Toldt. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the right side elevated, allowing the hepatic flexure to be retracted and mobilized. The transverse colon is mobilized up to the splenic flexure by dividing the gastrocolic ligament. The patient is then repositioned to Trendelenburg with the left side elevated.

The surgeon grasps the mesentery of the sigmoid colon with a Babcock clamp and retracts it towards the midline, identifying the left ureter. The sigmoid and descending colon are mobilized from the rectosigmoid junction to the splenic flexure. The patient is then positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the left side up, and the splenic flexure is taken down by dividing the splenocolic, gastrocolic, pancreatocolic, and colicoabdominal wall attachments.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire colon and retracts the rectum to the right using a Babcock clamp, identifying a vascular plane between the mesorectum and pelvic wall. The rectum is mobilized along the posterior wall up to the pelvic floor. The position is changed to right side up, retracting the rectum to the left, and identifying the right ureter. The right rectal stack is mobilized along the posterior wall. The uterus is tacked to the anterior abdominal wall to access the anterior rectal wall. Using an articulated linear endostapler, the rectum is transected just above the dentate line, with digital rectal and vaginal exams ensuring no vaginal wall is grasped before firing the stapler.

The surgeon takes down the mesenteries of the sigmoid, descending, transverse, and ascending colon. The specimen is delivered through the port site, and the terminal ileum is transected approximately five to eight centimeters from the ileocecal junction. A fifteen-centimeter J-pouch is created extracorporeally, and an anvil from a twenty-eight millimeter circular GIA device is inserted. The pouch is returned to the abdominal cavity, pneumoperitoneum is reestablished, and the stapler is fired to create the ileal pouch and anastomosis. An air bubble test is performed, the abdominal cavity is washed out, and a loop ileostomy is created at the port site.
